file_name: Zounds Hearing, Inc. - MANUFACTURING DESIGN MARKETING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: This MANUFACTURING, DESIGN AND MARKETING AGREEMENT (this "Agreement") is entered into by and between Zounds Hearing, Inc., a Delaware corporation ("Subcontractor") and InnerScope Hearing Technologies, Inc., a Nevada corporation, (the "Manufacturer") Manufacturer dated effective October 3, 2018 (the "Effective Date"). Subcontractor and Manufacturer may also be referred to herein individually as "Party" or collectively as the "Parties".
perturbation.changed_text: This MANUFACTURING, DESIGN AND MARKETING AGREEMENT (this "Agreement") is entered into by and between Zounds Hearing, Inc., a Delaware corporation ("Subcontractor") and InnerScope Hearing Technologies, Inc., a Nevada corporation, (the "Client") Client dated effective October 3, 2018 (the "Effective Date"). Subcontractor and Client may also be referred to herein individually as "Party" or collectively as the "Parties".
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Manufacturer' is changed to 'Client'. This creates an in-text contradiction because the document consistently refers to 'Manufacturer' throughout subsequent sections, and now, the introductory definition is inconsistent. This is particularly relevant in clauses dealing with orders, product specifications, and liability where clarity is paramount. This pertubation creates contradiction in all the text refering to 'Manufacturer' after it. This create In-Text contradiction as the document refers to both, 'Client' and 'Manufacturer' without defining them
perturbation.contradicted_location: 3. Orders for Manufacturer's Products to be Manufactured by Subcontractor
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Orders for Manufacturer's Products to be Manufactured by Subcontractor 3.1 Purchase Orders: Excess Inventory. Manufacturer shall provide to Subcontractor a purchase order setting forth the proposed quantity of Manufacturer's Products to be purchased by Manufacturer and delivery dates, which purchase order will be subject to written acceptance by Subcontractor. All purchase orders are non-cancellable, and the Manufacturer shall pay for the purchaser orders as follows: (i) for purchase orders for less than or equal to a total of five hundred (500) units in any given calendar month, payment of fifty percent (50%) of Product Cost (as defined below) shall be made in full at the time the order is placed and the remaining balance paid in full before the Manufacturer's Products are shipped; and (ii) for purchase orders for more than five hundred (500) units in any given calendar month, payment of one hundred percent (100%) of Product Cost shall be made in full at the time the order is placed. Manufacturer's purchase orders accepted by Subcontractor and any forecast provided to Subcontractor by Manufacturer will constitute authorization for Subcontractor to procure product components to manufacture the Manufacturer's Products covered by such purchase orders based on their lead times. Subcontractor will advise Manufacturer of the lead time of components contained within their order and any forecast and Manufacturer will pay for the components prior to the Subcontractor placing orders for the components.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Manufacturer' to 'Client' creates a direct contradiction with the contradicted text. The contradicted text refers to 'Manufacturer's Products,' 'Manufacturer shall provide,' and 'purchase orders accepted by Subcontractor by Manufacturer'. The changed text redefines Manufacturer as Client. This makes the contradicted clauses unclear and potentially unenforceable, as it is no longer clear whether the obligations apply to the 'Client' as newly defined or the original 'Manufacturer' term. The perturbation explanation specifically states that it creates an in-text contradiction, by refering to both 'Client' and 'Manufacturer' without clearly defining them and leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The agreed upon delivery dates are based on the Subcontractor's projected lead time, current inventory, commitments and Subcontractor's advice. Manufacturer agrees and acknowledges that all shipment dates are firm delivery dates.
perturbation.changed_text: The agreed upon delivery dates are based on the Subcontractor's projected lead time, current inventory, commitments and Subcontractor's advice. Buyer agrees and acknowledges that all shipment dates are firm delivery dates.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Manufacturer' is changed to 'Buyer' within the Delivery and Shipping section. This creates confusion because the rest of the contract refers to 'Manufacturer'. If 'Buyer' isn't explicitly defined as synonymous with 'Manufacturer', it becomes unclear which entity is bound by the delivery terms.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Delivery and Shipping. The agreed upon delivery dates are based on the Subcontractor's projected lead time, current inventory, commitments and Subcontractor's advice. Buyer agrees and acknowledges that all shipment dates are firm delivery dates. All shipments shall be F.O.B. the manufacturing facility unless otherwise mutually agreed upon in writing. The method and route of shipment shall be at Subcontractor's discretion, unless Manufacturer supplies instructions in writing at least five days prior to shipment. In addition to the purchase price, Manufacturer shall pay any and all transportation charges (including insurance). The risk of loss of and title to the Manufacturer's Products pass to Manufacturer upon the receipt of the Manufacturer's Products by the carrier. Subcontractor is not responsible for any installation of Manufacturer's Products sold hereunder or delays caused by Manufacturer-specified suppliers.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Manufacturer' to 'Buyer' introduces a clear contradiction. The contradicted text explicitly states obligations for the 'Manufacturer' regarding shipping instructions, payment of transportation charges, and risk of loss. By changing 'Manufacturer' to 'Buyer' in the changed text, it creates ambiguity about which entity bears these responsibilities, directly conflicting with the obligations outlined in the contradicted text. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction', which states that using multiple terms interchangeably without defining them leads to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Orders: All orders whether for Manufacturer's Products or components or inventory to be used in the manufacture of Manufacturer's Products are non-cancellable.
perturbation.changed_text: Orders: All orders whether for Company Products or components or inventory to be used in the manufacture of Company Products are non-cancellable.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Manufacturer's Products' to 'Company Products' in Section 8.1 introduces ambiguity. The term 'Company' is not defined anywhere within the contract. This creates uncertainty as to which specific products are subject to the non-cancellable order clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.1 Orders: All orders whether for Company Products or components or inventory to be used in the manufacture of Company Products are non-cancellable.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Manufacturer's Products' with 'Company Products'. While the explanation suggests ambiguity because 'Company' is not defined, the contradicted text also uses 'Company Products'. Since both texts use the same undefined term, there is no new or stronger contradiction introduced. It is more accurate to say the underlying text already had an issue (which the change does not resolve), but the change itself does not create a contradiction. The Misaligned Terminology is present, but the modification doesn't worsen the in-text contradiction.
